2026-05-02.title: Worldle Answer Today — May 2, 2026
2026-05-02.summary: Today's Worldle silhouette is giving players trouble. Here's how to narrow it down using the directional hint and percentage distance, plus the mistakes that keep costing people their sixth guess.
2026-05-02.articleHtml:
Today's country hit different. If you've burned through four or five guesses already and the thing on your screen still looks like a vaguely familiar blob, you're not alone. The silhouette this week has been particularly unforgiving, and May 2nd's entry is no exception.

Where in the world is today's answer?

The directional arrow is pointing roughly southeast today, which narrows things down immediately. When the arrow tells you the target country lies in that direction from your last guess, you're not just learning distance—you're learning relative position. That means if you guessed Brazil in a previous round and the arrow points southeast, the answer isn't in South America. It's somewhere in Africa or the southern Indian Ocean region.

Check the percentage distance too. If you're seeing something in the 8,000+ kilometer range, you're nowhere close. But if you're landing in the 2,000-3,000km band, you're in the right neighborhood. Today's distance metrics suggest a country in southern or eastern Africa, or possibly a smaller nation tucked into a corner of Asia.

The silhouette shape is your real tell, though. Today's outline has a distinctive curved western border that hooks inward—think of how certain countries wrap around bodies of water or other nations. That curvature is a strong signal if you know your African geography or have spent time staring at Southeast Asian maps.

Reading the directional clues

Most players fixate on the percentage and ignore the arrow. That's a mistake. The arrow tells you direction; the percentage tells you distance. Together they paint a picture that neither gives you alone.

When the arrow points at a sharp angle—not straight north, south, east, or west—you're dealing with a diagonal relationship. This usually means the target country is in a different hemisphere or on a different continent entirely from your guess. If you guessed a European country and the arrow points southwest, you're probably looking at West Africa or a country in the Caribbean basin.

Today's directional hint is doing heavy lifting. If you haven't been paying attention to the arrow's angle, go back and look at your last two or three guesses. The arrow's been telling you something. You might have been guessing in entirely the wrong hemisphere.

One thing players overlook: the arrow direction changes based on where you are. A guess in Japan pointing toward Australia will show a very different arrow than a guess in Argentina pointing toward the same target. The game calculates direction from your current guess to the hidden country, not the other way around. That means your guesses create a trail of directional breadcrumbs—if you track them mentally, you can triangulate.

Mistakes that waste guesses

Starting with obvious large countries is tempting. Russia, Canada, the USA—everyone's done it. But large countries are terrible opening guesses because they give you almost nothing. The distance will be enormous, and the directional arrow will point vaguely toward half the planet. You've spent a guess and learned nothing useful.

Instead, start with a mid-sized country in a different region than where you think the answer might be. A country in West Africa, then one in Central America, then one in Southeast Asia. Within three guesses, the directional arrows will converge on a specific region. That's when the real game starts.

Another common mistake: ignoring the shape after the first wrong guess. Players see a wrong answer and immediately try a completely different country without thinking about why the shape looked the way it did. Today's silhouette has a distinctive feature—I'm not going to spoil it, but if you've seen it once, you'll recognize it on a second look. Study the silhouette before you guess again.

Finally, some players get fixated on the percentage and chase the lowest number without considering direction. A 1,500km guess in the wrong direction is worse than a 3,000km guess that's pointing at the right region. Distance matters, but direction is the compass. Get the direction right first, then optimize for distance.

Questions players keep asking

Does the game use capital cities or major cities for distance calculations?

It uses the geometric center of each country, not capitals. This trips people up when a country has an unusual shape—a long, thin nation might have its geometric center nowhere near its capital. Chile is the classic example: Santiago is in the middle of the country, but the geometric center is still somewhere in the Atacama. Don't assume your guess's center is where you think it is.

Why does the arrow sometimes point in a direction that seems wrong?

The game calculates direction using great-circle distance on a sphere, not flat-map projections. If you're using a standard Mercator map in your head, the angles can look weird. The shortest path around a sphere doesn't always match what flat-earters expect. Trust the arrow—it's doing the math correctly.

Is there an archive of past puzzles?

Yes, the archive goes back to the game's launch. If you want to study patterns—certain silhouettes that recur, specific distance ranges that correspond to particular regions—the archive is your friend. Some players use it to build mental maps of what specific distance/direction combinations look like. The archive is linked in the menu, and it's searchable by date if you want to revisit a specific day.

Can I use the hint system without spoiling the answer?

The hint system in Worldle is subtle. You can reveal one letter of the country name at a time, which is useful if you're down to two or three possibilities and just need to confirm which one it is. The directional and distance hints are always shown, but the name reveal is optional. Use it strategically—revealing too early takes away the satisfaction of solving it cold.

If you're stuck after five guesses, the first letter reveal is worth it. But try to get there without it first. The puzzle is more satisfying when you crack it yourself, and you'll learn the geography faster if you resist the hints on easy days. Save them for the ones that genuinely have you scratching your head.

2026-04-30.title: Worldle Answer Today - April 30, 2026
2026-04-30.summary: Stuck on today's Worldle? This page breaks down the puzzle, shares what to watch for, and explains how to use spoiler-free hints if you need them. Plus, the mistakes that trip most players up and answers to the questions people ask us every day.
2026-04-30.articleHtml:
Today's Worldle page at a glance

April 30, 2026. You've got six guesses to identify today's country. The game shows you a silhouette—no labels, no borders, just the shape. Each guess you make tells you how far away you are (in kilometers), which direction to head, and how close your guess is shape-wise. That's it. That's the whole game.

Today's puzzle is a medium-difficulty one. The silhouette has some distinctive curves that narrow things down if you know what to look for. If you're landing on this page, you're probably either looking for a little help or curious about what others are saying about today's country. Either way, you're in the right spot.

The archive shows this is the 1,461st Worldle puzzle since the game launched. If you've been playing since day one, congratulations on nearly four years of daily geography. If you're newer, the learning curve is gentler than you'd expect—you'll start recognizing shapes after a week or two.

What to pay attention to in today's Worldle puzzle

When you're staring at a country silhouette, it's tempting to jump straight to your best guess. Don't. Take thirty seconds first. Here's what experienced players look at before typing anything:

The coastline-to-landmass ratio. Islands and archipelagos are obvious, but pay attention to how much of the border is coastline versus land borders. Countries with long coastlines relative to their size behave differently than landlocked nations. Today's silhouette has about 60% coastline exposure, which immediately eliminates a chunk of possibilities.

The orientation. Worldle rotates countries randomly, so you can't rely on north-facing being "up." Look at the overall proportions instead. Is it wider than it is tall? Taller than wide? Roughly square? Today's country is wider than it is tall, which helps narrow things down considerably.

Peninsulas and bays. Those distinctive jutting pieces aren't random. They correspond to real geography. A deep indentation might indicate a major bay or inlet. A narrow extension could be a peninsula. Today's silhouette has a noticeable protrusion on the eastern edge that rules out several similar-sized countries.

Size context. Worldle gives you distance hints, which means the game has a sense of scale. If you're guessing a tiny country and getting a distance of 4,000 kilometers, you know something's off. Today's puzzle sits in the medium range—small enough to be tricky, large enough that most players recognize it within two or three guesses.

If you're still stuck after your first guess, the distance number tells you roughly how far off you are. The direction arrow tells you which way to move geographically. The shape similarity percentage tells you if you're in the right ballpark structurally. Use all three together, not just one.

How to use this page without spoiling more than you want

We get it. You want help, but not the whole answer handed to you. Here's how to use this page strategically:

If you want zero spoilers: Read the first two sections only. The "Today's Worldle page at a glance" gives you context without specifics. The "What to pay attention to" section teaches you to fish without catching the fish for you. These sections focus on technique and approach, not on today's specific country.

If you want a nudge, not the answer: The common mistakes section below can help you avoid wasting guesses. Knowing what not to do sometimes helps as much as knowing what to do. You might realize you're making one of those mistakes and correct course on your own.

If you want partial hints: The FAQ section sometimes contains clues disguised as general information. Look for the questions about specific features or recent puzzles. Sometimes the answer to "how does the distance hint work?" also happens to be relevant to today's puzzle if you think about it.

If you're completely stuck: The archive is there. We've been publishing daily Worldle answer pages since the game launched, and each one has context about that specific puzzle. You can browse older pages to see the format and eventually find today's answer if you need it. There's no judgment here—some days are harder than others, and geography is a big subject.

The key is that this page is designed to be useful at whatever level of help you need. Start with the least spoiler-heavy sections and work your way down if you need more.

Common mistakes players make on Worldle

After watching thousands of players tackle these puzzles (through comments, emails, and the occasional frantic late-night question), certain mistakes come up again and again. Here's how to avoid them:

Ignoring the shape similarity percentage. New players tend to focus on distance and direction and forget about the shape number. But shape similarity is often the fastest way to narrow things down. If you're guessing a country that's 8,000 kilometers away but has 85% shape similarity, something's wrong with your distance guess. The shape and distance should roughly correlate—if they don't, you're probably looking at the wrong region entirely.

Guessing obvious countries too early. Everyone thinks of Brazil or Australia on day one. But if you're guessing the most famous country in a region and getting 40% shape similarity and 3,000 kilometers away, you're wasting a guess. Start broad, then narrow. Your second or third guess should be more specific than your first, not less.

Not using the direction hint properly. The arrow tells you which direction to move from your guess to reach the answer. But players often misinterpret this. If you're guessing Spain and the arrow points southeast, the answer isn't necessarily southeast of Spain—it means you need to move in a generally southeastern direction from where Spain sits on the map. Context matters.

Forgetting about microstates. San Marino, Monaco, Vatican City—these exist and show up in Worldle. If you're getting very low distance numbers (under 100 kilometers) and still missing, you might be guessing a tiny country near a microstate. It happens more often than you'd think.

Guessing the same type of country repeatedly. If you've guessed three large African countries and haven't gotten close, don't guess a fourth. Step back. Are you reading the silhouette wrong? Is your first guess setting you on the wrong continent? The distance hint is there to recalibrate your thinking.

Overthinking the rotation. Yes, Worldle rotates countries randomly. But the relative proportions stay the same. A country that's wider than tall will still be wider than tall when rotated. Don't convince yourself a horizontally-oriented country is actually vertical just because it looks different than you're used to.

Questions players keep asking about Worldle

How does the distance hint actually work?

The distance number represents the distance between the center points of your guessed country and the answer country, measured in kilometers. It's a straight-line distance, not a travel distance—you're not measuring roads or borders, just how far apart the two countries are geographically. This means a country on the west coast of one continent and a country on the east coast of another continent might be closer in distance than two neighboring countries if those neighbors are on opposite sides of their shared border.

The game uses capital cities as the reference points for these calculations in most cases, though some versions use geographic centers. Either way, the principle remains the same: the number tells you roughly how far off you are, and the direction arrow tells you which way to move on the map.

Why does my shape similarity percentage seem wrong?

The shape similarity algorithm isn't perfect—it's comparing your guess to the answer using some geometric matching that prioritizes overall proportions over exact matching. A country that looks vaguely similar in shape (both long and thin, for example) might score higher than you'd expect. Meanwhile, two countries that look similar to your eye but have different aspect ratios might score lower than you'd think.

The shape percentage is most useful as a relative comparison (is this guess closer than my last one?) rather than an absolute measure. If you're going from 45% to 65% similarity, you're moving in the right direction. If you're going from 45% to 47%, you might be on the wrong track entirely.

Can I play yesterday's puzzle or past puzzles?

Worldle resets daily, and the puzzle for each day is fixed. Once midnight passes in your timezone, yesterday's puzzle is gone unless you saved your results or remember your guesses. However, the archive on this site contains answer pages for past puzzles going back to launch. You can browse those pages to see how past puzzles played out, though obviously that won't help you with today's puzzle.

Some players use the archive to practice on old puzzles they haven't seen. If you're one of those players, you might find it helpful to try solving an old puzzle before checking its answer page. That's a legitimate way to practice when you've already solved today's puzzle or when you want a challenge without time pressure.

Why does Worldle sometimes feel easier or harder on different days?

It's not your imagination. Some days the silhouette is immediately recognizable (large, distinctive countries tend to be easier). Other days you get a medium-sized country with generic proportions that could be several things. The difficulty varies based on which country the algorithm selects for that day, and that selection is random.

There are also regional biases. If the algorithm picks a country from a region you know well, it feels easy. If it picks a country from a region you don't know (Central Asia, anyone?), it feels hard. The game is objectively the same difficulty every day, but subjective difficulty depends heavily on your existing geography knowledge.

Is there a pattern to which countries show up more often?

The game uses random selection, so theoretically every country should appear with equal probability over a long enough timeframe. In practice, players have noticed some countries appear more frequently than others in certain time periods, but this is likely recency bias—you remember the times a frustrating country appeared more than the dozens of times unremarkable countries appeared.

If you're tracking your results, you might notice patterns in your own solving history. But those patterns say more about which countries you struggle with than about the game's selection algorithm. The game doesn't weight countries based on difficulty or popularity.

That's the page. You've got six guesses, some helpful hints, and now a better sense of what to look for. Good luck with today's puzzle.
